summary: McKenzie draws parallels between startup life in Columbus, Ohio and Central Japan, noting that both regions share similar challenges of being outside major tech hubs. He references an article about what Silicon Valley could learn from Columbus and finds it directly applicable to his experience in Nagoya. He also teases an upcoming long-form piece about how small independent software vendors can maximize web applications.
key_points: Startup challenges outside Silicon Valley are universal — what applies to Columbus, Ohio also applies to Central Japan
Nagoya is more comparable to Detroit than Silicon Valley as a tech ecosystem
Even well-known web platforms like Craigslist and Mixi have limited awareness outside tech-centric regions
McKenzie is developing ideas about the advantages small software businesses have with web apps
money_quotes: What works in Columbus, Ohio is also very true for life in Central Japan.
Even Nagoya is more of a Detroit than it is a Silicon Valley.
Nobody here knows what Craigslist is, either. Most don't even know Mixi, which is Myspace for Japan.
I suppose I should wait until I've actually got a web app to be giving advice about how to make web apps, though.
key_insight: The challenges and opportunities of building tech businesses outside major hubs are remarkably similar whether you're in the American Midwest or Central Japan.
